<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# AHS: Introduction - Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automated Highway System (AHS):** An Automated Highway System is a specially instrumented roadway infrastructure that employs coordinated vehicle–infrastructure integration technologies—including magnetic sensors, radar, lidar, computer vision, and dedicated short-range communication (DSRC)—to enable the fully autonomous longitudinal and lateral control of vehicles operating in platoons, thereby eliminating the need for active human driver intervention under defined operational design domains (ODD).

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, AHS sits at the apex of the **Vehicle Automation Hierarchy (Level 4–5)** as defined by the **SAE J3016** standard, where the "Dynamic Driving Task (DDT)" is performed entirely by the automated system across the full ODD of the specially equipped highway segment.

### 1.1.1 Canonical Sub-Components of an AHS

| Sub-System | Functional Role |
|---|---|
| **Roadway Infrastructure** | Magnetic markers, inductive loops, transponders, lane markers |
| **On-Board Vehicle Automation** | Throttle, brake, steering actuators under ECU control |
| **Communication Backbone** | DSRC / C-V2X for V2I and V2V messaging |
| **Centralized Traffic Management** | Supervisory AHS controller managing entry/exit and platoon formation |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Magnetic Train of Tomorrow"**
> Imagine a metro rail system, but the trains are your everyday cars, and the tracks are invisible magnetic strips embedded in the asphalt. Once a car enters the AHS-equipped lane, a **virtual magnetic cable** locks it into a high-speed, tightly-packed platoon—like beads on a string—where the lead car is "driven" by the infrastructure and every following car automatically copies the motion. You, the human, can sit back, read a book, and sip coffee. When you want to leave, the system gracefully hands control back to you at an exit ramp.

This is exactly the paradigm envisioned by the **California PATH (Partners for Advanced Transit and Highways) Program (1986–present)** at UC Berkeley, which demonstrated 8–10 vehicle platoons travelling at **96 km/h (60 mph)** with inter-vehicle gaps of approximately **6.5 m (21 ft)** in 1997.

### 1.2.1 Historical Milestones (Quick Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> - **1986** — U.S. FHWA launches the **NAHSC (National Automated Highway System Consortium)** as part of the ISTEA legislation.
> - **1997** — Demo '97 in San Diego, California: 20 AHS-equipped vehicles (Buick LeSabres, Honda Accords) demonstrate platooning on I-15.
> - **2004 onwards** — DARPA Grand Challenges catalyze the shift toward **Autonomous Vehicles (AVs)** rather than infrastructure-heavy AHS.
> - **2010s–2020s** — Industry pivot to **V2X-connected autonomous vehicles**, with AHS principles absorbed into modern Cooperative Driving Automation (CDA).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **AHS** with **Autonomous Vehicles (AVs)**. Remember: AHS = **Infrastructure + Vehicle cooperation**, while AVs = **Vehicle-only automation**. AHS does not require full AI-level intelligence inside each car because the *road* does most of the "thinking."

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following values are **standardized benchmarks** students must memorize for the KTU examination:
> - **Inter-vehicle gap in platoon:** **6.5 m (21 ft)**
> - **Platoon length:** typically **8–10 vehicles**
> - **Operating speed on AHS lane:** **96 km/h (60 mph)**
> - **Communication frequency (DSRC):** **5.85–5.925 GHz** (U.S. FCC allocation)
> - **Latency budget for longitudinal control:** **≤ 100 ms**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# AHS: Introduction - Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Decomposition

The AHS operational logic is best understood as a **four-stage functional cascade**:

### Step 1 — Check-In (Entry)
- As a vehicle approaches the AHS entrance ramp, a **roadside beacon** interrogates the vehicle's **transponder**.
- Validation checks: vehicle is AHS-equipped, mechanically sound, software version current.
- AHS controller issues a **handshake acknowledgment** and assigns a **platoon slot ID**.

### Step 2 — Platoon Formation
- The vehicle merges into the dedicated AHS lane and aligns with an existing platoon or initiates a new one.
- **Why this matters:** Platooning converts the **aerodynamic drag** problem of single vehicles into a **slipstream economy**—fuel savings of up to **20%** have been empirically measured.

### Step 3 — Automated Operation (Cruise Phase)
- Longitudinal control (speed, gap) maintained via **look-ahead radar**, **lidar**, and **V2V beacon data**.
- Lateral control (lane-keeping) maintained via **magnetic markers** buried every **1.2 m** in the road surface, sensed by on-board **magnetometers**.
- All decisions are sub-second, deterministic, and bounded by **formal safety envelopes**.

### Step 4 — Check-Out (Exit)
- The vehicle signals intent to leave; the system opens a **gap-maneuver window**.
- Lateral control smoothly transfers to the human driver, with a **minimum 5-second takeover request** displayed on the HMI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the infrastructure "drive" instead of each car?**
> Because in 1990s-era AHS (PATH philosophy), on-board computation was expensive, unreliable, and limited. Off-loading path planning to the roadside computer simplified vehicle hardware, traded off against costly infrastructure investment. Modern V2X systems blur this line by distributing intelligence, but the *principle* of cooperative perception remains.

---

## 2.2 The Three Layers of AHS Architecture

| Layer | Name | Function | Example Technology |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | **Physical Layer** | Sensors, actuators, road markers | Magnetic nails, radar, DSRC antenna |
| **L2** | **Communication Layer** | V2V / V2I data exchange | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p), C-V2X (3GPP Rel. 14) |
| **L3** | **Control & Management Layer** | Platoon control, traffic management | AHS supervisor, ECU logic |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are examinable in the KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE).** Memorize the variables, units, and physical meaning.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Variables | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{latency} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{comm} + T_{actuate}$ | $T$ in **ms** | Safety envelope design |
| 2 | $G_{safe} = v \cdot T_{latency} + d_{margin}$ | $G_{safe}$ = safe gap, $v$ = velocity, $d_{margin}$ = **2 m** buffer | Platoon spacing law |
| 3 | $D_{platoon} = (n - 1) \cdot g + L_{veh}$ | $D$ = platoon length, $n$ = vehicles, $g$ = gap, $L_{veh}$ = vehicle length | Roadway capacity gain |
| 4 | $C_{AHS} = \dfrac{v \cdot 3600}{D_{platoon} + D_{headway}}$ | $C$ = lane capacity (veh/hr/lane) | Throughput maximization |
| 5 | $S_{fuel} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{C_D^{platoon}}{C_D^{single}}\right)$ | $C_D$ = drag coefficient | Energy efficiency analysis |
| 6 | $R_{coop} = \dfrac{N_{vehicles}^{connected}}{N_{vehicles}^{total}}$ | $R$ = cooperation ratio (0 to 1) | Market penetration metric |
| 7 | $a_{max} = \mu \cdot g_{accel} - \dfrac{F_{drag}}{m}$ | $a$ = max deceleration, $\mu$ = friction, $m$ = mass | Emergency braking limit |
| 8 | $\omega_{control} = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_{control\;loop}}$ | $\omega$ = control bandwidth (rad/s) | Control loop design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formula Sheet Care:** In the markdown table above, vertical bars (e.g., for $C_D \vert_{platoon}$) have been intentionally avoided to prevent markdown parser breakage. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` when writing KTU exam answer scripts in LaTeX.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Roadway Capacity:** A single AHS lane can theoretically carry **4,000–6,000 vehicles/hour**, versus **2,000 vehicles/hour** on a conventional lane—effectively doubling capacity without adding pavement.
- **Safety:** Human error causes **~94%** of crashes (NHTSA, 2015). AHS removes the human from the control loop in the ODD, theoretically reducing crash rates toward **near-zero**.
- **Energy:** Platoon fuel savings of **10–20%** at highway speeds; non-trivial for freight logistics.
- **Land Use:** Doubles capacity without eminent domain—politically and economically valuable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# AHS: Introduction - Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Safe Longitudinal Gap Equation

This derivation shows **why a 6.5 m platoon gap is both safe and efficient** under the canonical 100 ms latency envelope.

### Starting Premise (Newtonian Kinematics)

During the latency window $T_{latency}$, a following vehicle must not close the gap. The minimum safe gap $G_{safe}$ must therefore exceed the distance the relative motion could consume.

$$
G_{safe} = v \cdot T_{latency} + d_{margin}
$$

### Step-by-Step Expansion

**Step 1:** Define each term explicitly.

$$
T_{latency} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{comm} + T_{actuate}
$$

Each sub-term has a physical meaning:
- $T_{sense}$ = time for radar/lidar to register the lead vehicle's position
- $T_{compute}$ = time for the ECU to compute the corrective throttle/brake command
- $T_{comm}$ = time for the command signal to traverse the CAN bus
- $T_{actuate}$ = time for the brake caliper or throttle motor to physically respond

**Step 2:** Substitute empirical values from PATH Demo '97.

$$
T_{sense} = 20\,\text{ms},\quad T_{compute} = 30\,\text{ms},\quad T_{comm} = 10\,\text{ms},\quad T_{actuate} = 40\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the components.

$$
T_{latency} = 20 + 30 + 10 + 40 = 100\,\text{ms} = 0.1\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 4:** At the canonical AHS cruise speed of $v = 96$ km/h, convert to SI units.

$$
v = 96 \times \dfrac{1000}{3600} = 26.67\,\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the gap formula with $d_{margin} = 2$ m (standard PATH margin).

$$
G_{safe} = 26.67 \times 0.1 + 2 = 2.667 + 2 = 4.67\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 6:** Engineering rounding upward to account for sensor noise and tire slip yields the **6.5 m** canonical figure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of Derivation 1:** The 6.5 m platoon gap used in AHS is **not arbitrary**—it is the engineering-rounded upper bound on a 100 ms latency envelope at 96 km/h, with a 2 m safety margin. This is exactly the type of derivation the KTU examiner may expect in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — AHS Lane Capacity vs. Conventional Lane

This derivation proves **how AHS doubles lane capacity**—a frequently tested concept.

### Conventional Lane Capacity (HCM 2016 Baseline)

The Highway Capacity Manual gives:

$$
C_{conv} = \dfrac{3600 \cdot v}{D_{headway}^{conv}}
$$

For a single car with bumper-to-bumper spacing of approximately **$D_{headway}^{conv} = 40$ m** (≈ 2-second rule at 72 km/h):

$$
C_{conv} = \dfrac{3600 \times 20}{40} = 1800\,\text{veh/hr/lane}
$$

This matches the empirical **2,000 veh/hr/lane** figure.

### AHS Lane Capacity

For an 8-vehicle platoon, with gap $g = 6.5$ m and $L_{veh} = 4.5$ m, the platoon length is:

$$
D_{platoon} = (n - 1) \cdot g + L_{veh} = 7 \times 6.5 + 4.5 = 45.5 + 4.5 = 50\,\text{m}
$$

Adding a **headway between platoons** of $D_{headway}^{AHS} = 10$ m (i.e., 0.36 s at 96 km/h for safety), the effective spacing is:

$$
D_{eff} = D_{platoon} + D_{headway}^{AHS} = 50 + 10 = 60\,\text{m}
$$

Converting to capacity:

$$
C_{AHS} = \dfrac{3600 \times 26.67}{60} = 1600\,\text{veh/hr/platoon}
$$

But the AHS system can stack **8 vehicles in 50 m**, meaning per-meter throughput is:

$$
\rho_{AHS} = \dfrac{n}{D_{platoon}} = \dfrac{8}{50} = 0.16\,\text{veh/m}
$$

Compared to conventional:

$$
\rho_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{40} = 0.025\,\text{veh/m}
$$

**Capacity ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{\rho_{AHS}}{\rho_{conv}} = \dfrac{0.16}{0.025} = 6.4
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of Derivation 2:** AHS provides up to **6.4× the linear density** of a conventional lane. When adjusted for safe inter-platoon headway, the practical lane capacity is **2–3× the conventional lane**—aligning with the well-cited "double the capacity" engineering claim.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — AHS vs. Autonomous Vehicles (AV) vs. ADAS

| Dimension | AHS | AV (SAE L4–L5) | ADAS (L1–L3) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Automation Locus** | Infrastructure-led | Vehicle-led | Driver-led |
| **Required Road Mods** | High (magnetic markers) | None | None |
| **V2X Dependence** | Mandatory | Optional | None |
| **Failure Fallback** | Hand back to driver | Minimal-risk maneuver | Driver immediate |
| **Cost Driver** | Roadway CAPEX | Vehicle CAPEX | Vehicle CAPEX (low) |
| **Capacity Gain** | **2–3×** | Marginal | None |
| **Real-World Example** | PATH Demo '97 | Waymo, Cruise | Tesla Autopilot |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to "compare" in 14-mark questions, *use a 2-column or 3-column table* in your answer script. Examiners award full marks for structured comparative analysis rather than paragraph essays.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# AHS: Introduction - Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — AHS Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Human Driver] --> B{Vehicle AHS Equipped?}
    B -- No --> Z1[Manual Lane: Conventional Driving]
    B -- Yes --> C[Check-In Beacon: Roadside Transponder]
    C --> D[AHS Supervisor: Authentication and Slot Assignment]
    D --> E[Platoon Formation Coordinator]
    E --> F[Platoon Lead Vehicle]
    E --> G[Follower Vehicle Slot 2]
    E --> H[Follower Vehicle Slot 3]
    E --> I[Follower Vehicle Slot N]
    F --> J[Sensor Fusion Engine]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Longitudinal and Lateral Controller}
    K --> L[Throttle Actuator]
    K --> M[Brake Actuator]
    K --> N[Steering Actuator]
    L --> O[Vehicle Dynamics]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[DSRC V2V Beacon]
    O --> Q[DSRC V2I Beacon]
    P --> R[Platoon State Update Broadcast]
    Q --> S[Roadside Traffic Manager]
    S --> T{Driver Check-Out Request?}
    T -- No --> K
    T -- Yes --> U[Gap Maneuver and Lane Exit]
    U --> V[Control Handoff to Driver]
    V --> W[Driver: Manual Mode Resumed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow above traces a vehicle from the moment it approaches an AHS entrance, through its check-in, platoon joining, automated cruise, and final check-out. The **central nervous system** of the architecture is the **Sensor Fusion Engine (J)**, which feeds the **Controller (K)**, which commands three physical actuators (L, M, N).

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — AHS Operational Phases (Sequential State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Manual
    Manual --> CheckIn: AHS Lane Approached
    CheckIn --> Validating: Transponder Handshake
    Validating --> Rejected: Auth Failed
    Rejected --> Manual: Return to Manual Mode
    Validating --> PlatoonJoin: Auth OK
    PlatoonJoin --> Cruise: Slot Assigned
    Cruise --> EmergencyBrake: Hazard Detected
    EmergencyBrake --> Manual: After Stop
    Cruise --> CheckOut: Exit Signal Received
    CheckOut --> Manual: Hand-Off Complete
    Manual --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the State Diagram:** This is a formal **Mealy-style state machine** suitable for KTU questions on "Explain the operational phases of AHS." Each state is a distinct mode of vehicle behavior; transitions are triggered by explicit events (e.g., "Exit Signal Received").

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Platoon Topology (Vehicle-to-Vehicle Mesh)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Lead Vehicle] -->|V2V Beacon 10Hz| F1[Follower 1]
    F1 -->|V2V Beacon 10Hz| F2[Follower 2]
    F2 -->|V2V Beacon 10Hz| F3[Follower 3]
    F3 -->|V2V Beacon 10Hz| F4[Follower 4]
    F1 -.->|Gap Probe 50Hz| L
    F2 -.->|Gap Probe 50Hz| F1
    F3 -.->|Gap Probe 50Hz| F2
    F4 -.->|Gap Probe 50Hz| F3
    classDef lead fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef follower fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class L lead
    class F1,F2,F3,F4 follower
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Platoon Diagram:** Solid arrows are **state broadcasts** (10 Hz, low data rate). Dotted arrows are **gap-probe pings** (50 Hz, high frequency, used for real-time longitudinal control). The lead vehicle is shaded gold; followers are shaded sky-blue.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — AHS Information Flow Timeline (Gantt-Style)

```mermaid
gantt
    title AHS Check-In to Cruise Timeline
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s s
    section Vehicle A
    Approach        :a1, 0, 2s
    Transponder    :a2, after a1, 1s
    Lane Merge     :a3, after a2, 3s
    Platoon Sync   :a4, after a3, 2s
    Cruise Steady  :a5, after a4, 10s
    section AHS Infra
    Auth Request   :b1, 0, 1s
    Slot Assign    :b2, after b1, 1s
    Platoon Ctrl   :b3, after b2, 5s
    Steady State   :b4, after b3, 10s
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Gantt:** This timeline shows the **synchronization required** between the vehicle (Section A) and the infrastructure (Section B). The infrastructure's slot assignment must complete before the vehicle's lane merge begins—otherwise the vehicle has nowhere to merge into.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# AHS: Introduction - Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Automated Highway System (AHS). List any two infrastructure components required for its operation.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** An Automated Highway System is a roadway system that uses vehicle–infrastructure integration to provide fully automatic longitudinal and lateral control of vehicles, eliminating the need for active human driver input within the equipped section.
>
> **Infrastructure Components (1 mark, any two):**
> 1. Magnetic markers embedded in the pavement for lateral reference.
> 2. Roadside transponders / DSRC beacons for check-in and V2I communication.
> 3. Inductive loops for vehicle detection at entry/exit zones.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the 6.5 m inter-vehicle gap in AHS platoons?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The 6.5 m inter-vehicle gap is the **engineered minimum safe distance** derived from a **100 ms end-to-end latency budget** (sensing + compute + communication + actuation) at a cruise speed of **96 km/h**, with an additional **2 m safety margin**. It enables tight platooning for aerodynamic fuel savings (**~20%**) while maintaining safety, and is a key enabler of the 2–3× lane capacity gain.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Set A]`

**a)** Explain the **operational concept of an Automated Highway System (AHS)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **Check-In, Platoon Formation, Cruise, and Check-Out** phases. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With relevant derivations, prove that an AHS-equipped lane provides **at least 2× the capacity** of a conventional lane. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Phase 1 — Check-In (2 marks):**
- The vehicle approaches the AHS entrance ramp.
- A roadside beacon interrogates the on-board transponder.
- Authentication, version check, mechanical fitness check are performed.
- The AHS supervisor assigns a **platoon slot ID** and a **merge time window**.

**Phase 2 — Platoon Formation (2 marks):**
- The vehicle merges into the AHS lane at the prescribed merge point.
- It accelerates / decelerates to match the lead vehicle's speed (typically **96 km/h**).
- Once matched, it locks into a **6.5 m gap** behind the preceding vehicle.
- The platoon is now a coordinated unit, with V2V beacons broadcasting state at **10 Hz**.

**Phase 3 — Cruise (2 marks):**
- The longitudinal controller maintains the gap using radar/lidar gap-probes at **50 Hz**.
- The lateral controller tracks magnetic markers buried every **1.2 m** in the road.
- The driver is in a **"hands-off, mind-off"** state within the ODD.

**Phase 4 — Check-Out (1 mark):**
- The driver signals exit intent via HMI.
- The system opens a **gap-maneuver window** and gradually widens the gap.
- Control is **handed back to the human** with a **≥ 5 second** warning.
- The vehicle re-enters the manual driving mode on a conventional lane.

**(Block Diagram 2 marks):** As in Section 4.1 of these notes.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Define Conventional Capacity (1 mark):**
Using HCM baseline values, $v = 72$ km/h, $D_{headway} = 40$ m:

$$
C_{conv} = \dfrac{3600 \times 20}{40} = 1800\,\text{veh/hr/lane}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Platoon Length (2 marks):**
For 8 vehicles, $g = 6.5$ m, $L_{veh} = 4.5$ m:

$$
D_{platoon} = (8 - 1) \times 6.5 + 4.5 = 45.5 + 4.5 = 50\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Effective Per-Platoon Spacing (1 mark):**
With $D_{headway}^{AHS} = 10$ m:

$$
D_{eff} = 50 + 10 = 60\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Linear Density Comparison (2 marks):**

$$
\rho_{AHS} = \dfrac{8}{50} = 0.16\,\text{veh/m}
$$

$$
\rho_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{40} = 0.025\,\text{veh/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Capacity Ratio (1 mark):**

$$
\dfrac{\rho_{AHS}}{\rho_{conv}} = 6.4
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion with Assumptions (1 mark):**
Adjusted for safe inter-platoon headway, AHS provides **2–3× practical capacity gain**. Assumptions: uniform vehicle length, single-lane analysis, no merging disruption, 100% AHS market penetration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Set B]`

**a)** Compare and contrast **Automated Highway Systems (AHS)** with **Autonomous Vehicles (AV)**. Use a comparative matrix covering automation locus, infrastructure dependence, V2X requirement, capacity gain, and cost driver. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Derive the **safe longitudinal gap equation** $G_{safe} = v \cdot T_{latency} + d_{margin}$ from first principles, and show that at the canonical AHS cruise parameters, the gap evaluates to approximately **4.67 m**, justifying the engineering-rounded **6.5 m** figure. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Comparative Matrix (5 marks):**

| Dimension | AHS | AV (SAE L4–L5) |
|---|---|---|
| Automation Locus | Infrastructure-led | Vehicle-led |
| Infrastructure Dependence | **High** (magnetic markers, beacons) | **Low** (uses on-board sensors only) |
| V2X Requirement | **Mandatory** (DSRC/C-V2X essential) | **Optional** (best-effort enhancement) |
| Lane Capacity Gain | **2–3×** (due to platooning) | **Marginal** (single-vehicle autonomy) |
| Cost Driver | Roadway CAPEX | Vehicle CAPEX (sensors, compute) |

**Justification (2 marks):**
- AHS achieves capacity gain through **platooning**, which AVs alone cannot replicate without V2X.
- AVs require **no road modifications**, making them politically and economically easier to deploy at scale.
- AHS is more **deterministic and verifiable** because the control logic resides in the infrastructure, but its high CAPEX led to industry abandonment in favor of AVs.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Define the latency budget (2 marks):**

$$
T_{latency} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{comm} + T_{actuate}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute PATH Demo '97 values (1 mark):**

$$
T_{latency} = 20 + 30 + 10 + 40 = 100\,\text{ms} = 0.1\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert cruise speed to SI (1 mark):**

$$
v = 96\,\text{km/h} = 26.67\,\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply safe gap equation (1 mark):**

$$
G_{safe} = v \cdot T_{latency} + d_{margin}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate (1 mark):**

$$
G_{safe} = 26.67 \times 0.1 + 2 = 4.67\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Justify engineering rounding (1 mark):**
The 4.67 m theoretical minimum must be **inflated to 6.5 m** in practice to accommodate:
- Sensor noise (radar/lidar $\pm 0.5$ m)
- Tire slip in wet conditions
- Wireless packet loss (DSRC requires 99.9% reliability)
- Brake wear variation

Thus **6.5 m is the safe, conservative, engineering-rounded value**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in AHS Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing AHS with AVs** — Examiners will instantly deduct 1–2 marks if you use these terms interchangeably. Always clarify that **AHS = infrastructure-led**, **AV = vehicle-led**.
> 2. **Skipping units in derivations** — Every numerical substitution **must show units**. A derivation that yields "4.67" without "meters" loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting assumptions** — Capacity and gap derivations **always require explicit assumptions** (e.g., "uniform vehicle length," "100% AHS penetration," "no merging disruption"). Omitting these loses 1 mark minimum.
> 4. **Ignoring safety margins** — The 6.5 m figure is **not equal to** the 4.67 m theoretical minimum. Examiners specifically look for the **engineering rounding** justification.
> 5. **Hand-waving the latency budget** — The 100 ms latency must be **decomposed** into its 4 sub-components (sense, compute, comm, actuate). A monolithic "100 ms" answer loses marks.
> 6. **No diagram for 7-mark conceptual questions** — A neat labeled block diagram is **mandatory** for full marks on operational-concept questions. Skipping the diagram forfeits up to 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AHS: Introduction — Last-Minute Revision Pack**

- [ ] **AHS Definition:** Infrastructure-led full automation using V2X, magnetic markers, and on-board actuators.
- [ ] **AHS ≠ AV:** AHS uses road infrastructure; AVs use only vehicle sensors.
- [ ] **SAE Level:** AHS operates at **SAE Level 4–5** within its ODD.
- [ ] **Platoon gap:** **6.5 m** (canonical, from PATH Demo '97).
- [ ] **Platoon size:** **8–10 vehicles**.
- [ ] **Cruise speed:** **96 km/h (60 mph)**.
- [ ] **Latency budget:** **100 ms** = 20 + 30 + 10 + 40 ms (sense + compute + comm + actuate).
- [ ] **DSRC frequency:** **5.85–5.925 GHz** (U.S. FCC).
- [ ] **Capacity gain:** **2–3×** conventional lane (up to 6.4× linear density).
- [ ] **Fuel saving:** **~20%** in platoon (slipstream effect).
- [ ] **Four operational phases:** Check-In → Platoon Formation → Cruise → Check-Out.
- [ ] **Three architecture layers:** Physical, Communication, Control & Management.
- [ ] **Magnetic marker spacing:** **1.2 m** along the road centerline.
- [ ] **V2V beacon rate:** **10 Hz** state, **50 Hz** gap probe.
- [ ] **Takeover warning time:** **≥ 5 seconds** before manual handoff.
- [ ] **Historical landmark:** **Demo '97, San Diego, I-15** — 20 AHS vehicles.
- [ ] **Safety envelope:** AHS targets the **94% crash reduction** opportunity by removing human error.
- [ ] **Key derivation #1:** $G_{safe} = v \cdot T_{latency} + d_{margin}$ → **4.67 m** → rounded to **6.5 m**.
- [ ] **Key derivation #2:** $\rho_{AHS} / \rho_{conv} = 6.4$ → 2–3× practical capacity.
- [ ] **Critical Pitfall:** Always state **assumptions** and **units** in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts and Technologies of AHS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET721 - INTELLIGENT TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Concepts and Technologies of AHS # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Automated Highway System (AHS)? An **Automated Highway System (AHS)** is a specially instrumented roadway infrastructure combined with cooperating, automated vehicles that operate as a fully integrated, self-governing transportation system. In an AHS, the driving tasks traditionally performed by humans — steering, acceleration, braking, lane changing, and obstacle avoidance — are transferred either partially or fully to an intelligent **machine-to-machine (M2M)** control loop operating between the vehicle, the roadside, and other vehicles. Formally, as defined by the **United States Department of Transportation (USDOT)** and the **National Automated Highway System Consortium (NAHSC)**, an AHS is: > *"A system that provides for the fully automatic control of motor vehicles on a dedicated lane or set of lanes, with the intent of improving safety, increasing traffic throughput, reducing environmental impact, and enhancing driver comfort."* The AHS concept is a direct sub-domain under **Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS)**, sitting at the highest tier of vehicle automation (NHTSA Level 4–5). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OECET721 Module 4):** > AHS forms the technological culmination of ITS — where **roadside infrastructure**, **in-vehicle intelligence**, and **wireless communication** converge to remove the human driver from the immediate control loop on defined highway segments. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driverless Train on Rubber Wheels" Imagine an **urban metro rail system** that runs on dedicated tracks, has no human driver, follows a precise schedule, maintains a tiny headway (gap) from the train ahead, stops exactly at platform doors, and adjusts its speed automatically based on traffic. Now, replace the steel rails with an **instrumented asphalt lane**, the train cars with **cars and trucks**, and the trackside signalling system with **wireless beacons and cameras**. That is exactly what an AHS does. The key insight is this: **a road is to a normal car what a rail track is to a metro train.** AHS effectively turns the highway lane into a "virtual rail" using **magnetic markers, radar reflectors, machine vision, and V2V communication**, allowing vehicles to lock onto the lane and follow it with millimetric precision — far better than a human driver can. ### 1.3 Why AHS? The Core Driving Forces AHS is not built for novelty; it is built to solve four deep, structural problems of modern road transport: | Problem in Conventional Highways | How AHS Solves It | |---|---| | Human reaction time (~1.5 s) limits safe car-following gap | Electronic reaction time (~0.1 s) enables **platooning** at very small gaps | | Human lane-keeping accuracy is ~0.5 m | Machine vision + steering control achieves **<0.05 m** lateral accuracy | | Crashes are mostly caused by **human error (~94%)** | Removes the human from the loop → eliminates fatigue, distraction, DUI | | Highway capacity capped at ~2,200 veh/hr/lane | AHS can theoretically push capacity to **6,000–8,000 veh/hr/lane** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Standardized Metric (NHTSA / SAE J3016):** > The industry-standard **0–5 automation scale** classifies AHS-equipped vehicles at **Level 4 (High Automation)** or **Level 5 (Full Automation)**. Levels 0–2 are *driver-assist* only, not AHS. ### 1.4 The Three Pillars of AHS Every AHS deployment rests on three interdependent pillars: 1. **Smart Vehicle (Automated Vehicle Side)** — Onboard sensors, actuators, controllers, and software. 2. **Smart Infrastructure (Roadside Side)** — Roadway instrumentation such as magnetic markers, inductive loops, beacons, edge computing, and digital signage. 3. **Smart Communication (Linking Layer)** — Dedicated Short-Range Communications (DSRC), Cellular-V2X (C-V2X), and 5G networks enabling Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V), Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I), and Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) data exchange. > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-line definition to memorize for KTU exams:** > *"An AHS is a system where intelligent vehicles operate on an instrumented highway using machine-to-machine communication, performing all longitudinal and lateral control functions automatically."* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet ## 2.1 Levels of Vehicle Automation — The NHTSA / SAE J3016 Framework To understand *what* an AHS is, you must first classify *how much* of the driving task is automated. The **SAE J3016** standard (adopted globally and referenced by KTU) defines **six levels of driving automation**. | Level | Name | Steering / Acceleration / Braking | Environment Monitoring | Fallback Performance | AHS Relevance | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | No Automation | Human | Human | Human | Not AHS | | 1 | Driver Assistance | Human OR System (one function) | Human | Human | Not AHS | | 2 | Partial Automation | System (both) | Human | Human | Not AHS — e.g., Tesla Autopilot (basic) | | 3 | Conditional Automation | System | System | Human | Approaching AHS — e.g., Traffic Jam Pilot | | 4 | High Automation | System | System | System | **Core AHS band — geofenced** | | 5 | Full Automation | System | System | System | **Theoretical full AHS — any road** | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Hook:** AHS is fundamentally a **Level 4** system. The highway acts as the "Operational Design Domain (ODD)" — outside it, the vehicle may revert to a lower level or stop safely. ## 2.2 The Four Functional Layers of an AHS Architecture The architecture of any AHS implementation can be decomposed into four stacked functional layers. KTU examiners love asking about this hierarchy. ### Layer 1 — Perception Layer ("Sensing") This is where the AHS "sees" the world. - **Onboard sensors:** Cameras (machine vision), Radar (mmWave 76–81 GHz), LiDAR (905 nm / 1550 nm pulsed laser), Ultrasonic, GNSS/IMU. - **Roadside sensors:** Inductive loops, overhead radar, CCTV with AI analytics. ### Layer 2 — Communication Layer ("Talking") Bidirectional data exchange using: - **V2V** (Vehicle-to-Vehicle) — peer data sharing - **V2I** (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure) — talking to roadside units (RSU) - **V2X** (Vehicle-to-Everything) — umbrella term - **Protocols:** DSRC (IEEE 802.11p), C-V2X (3GPP Release 14+), 5G sidelink ### Layer 3 — Control Layer ("Deciding & Acting") The brain of the AHS — split into: - **Longitudinal Control:** Throttle and brake actuators maintain safe spacing, headway, and platoon formation. - **Lateral Control:** Steering actuator keeps the vehicle precisely within the lane envelope. ### Layer 4 — Coordination Layer ("Organizing") Higher-level intelligence: - Platoon formation/dissolution - Merging/diverging algorithms - Lane-change negotiation - Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control (CACC) logic > [!IMPORTANT] > **For 14-mark KTU questions:** Always structure your AHS explanation using these four layers. Examiners award marks for "systematic layered description." ## 2.3 AHS Operational Sub-Systems (High-Yield Concept Sheet) | Sub-System | Function | Enabling Technology | KTU Tag | |---|---|---|---| | **Lane-Keeping Assist (LKA)** | Steers vehicle within lane boundaries | Machine vision + steering actuator | Lateral Control | | **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** | Maintains safe time-headway from preceding vehicle | Forward radar | Longitudinal Control | | **Cooperative ACC (CACC)** | Uses V2V data to reduce gap further | DSRC / C-V2X + radar | Longitudinal + V2V | | **Platooning** | Tightly-coupled vehicle string with <1 m gaps | CACC + V2V + braking coordination | Coordination | | **Automated Lane Changing** | Executes safe lane-change manoeuvres | 360° sensors + V2V + decision algo. | Coordination | | **Automated Merging** | Smooth entry onto automated lane | V2I + trajectory planning | Coordination | | **Obstacle Detection & Response** | Detects stationary/moving hazards | LiDAR + camera fusion | Perception | | **Highway Entry/Exit Hand-off** | Transitions vehicle between manual and AHS mode | HMI + controller handoff | Coordination | | **Localization** | Sub-meter positioning of vehicle on map | GNSS + IMU + HD maps | Perception | | **Roadside Infrastructure** | Magnetic markers, beacons, RSUs | Magnetometers, RFID, DSRC | Infrastructure | ## 2.4 Key Communication & Sensing Equations for AHS While AHS is largely a systems topic, KTU frequently tests the following equations. ### 2.4.1 Safe Time-Headway (Longitudinal Control) The fundamental spacing rule used by ACC/CACC controllers: $$d_{safe} = v \cdot t_h + d_0$$ Where: - $d_{safe}$ = required safe following distance (m) - $v$ = vehicle speed (m/s) - $t_h$ = **time headway** (s) — typically 1.4 s for ACC, **0.6 s for CACC, 0.2 s for tight platoons** - $d_0$ = minimum standstill gap (m), often **2 m** ### 2.4.2 Doppler Radar Range Equation (Perception) $$P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot R^4 \cdot L}$$ Where: - $P_r$ = received power (W) - $P_t$ = transmitted power (W) - $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains - $\lambda$ = wavelength (m) - $\sigma$ = radar cross-section of target (m²) - $R$ = range to target (m) - $L$ = system losses > [!NOTE] > **Why the 4th power of R matters:** Doubling the sensing distance reduces received power by **16×**. This is why AHS vehicles use 77 GHz mmWave radar — shorter wavelength $\lambda$ compensates for $R^4$ losses. ### 2.4.3 DSRC Communication Latency Target $$T_{latency} \leq 100 \text{ ms}$$ For safety-critical AHS messages (e.g., emergency brake), the end-to-end latency from sensor trigger to receiver must be **less than 100 milliseconds** — equivalent to about **3 human reaction times compressed into one**. ### 2.4.4 Platoon Stability Constant For an $n$-vehicle string under constant-spacing policy: $$s_i(t) = (i-1) \cdot d_{gap}$$ Where $s_i$ is the position of the $i$-th vehicle and $d_{gap}$ is the intra-platoon gap (e.g., 4–10 m). String stability requires disturbance amplification **< 1** upstream — i.e., a braking event in the lead vehicle should not amplify in trailing vehicles. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often quote $T_{latency} \leq 10$ ms (this is for intra-platoon CACC control loops, not general V2X). The standard ITS-V2X target is **100 ms**; 5G URLLC targets **1 ms**. Mention the correct context. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of AHS | Engineering Field | Utility | |---|---| | Traffic Engineering | Triple highway capacity without new construction | | Automotive Industry | Foundation for autonomous commercial vehicles (trucking) | | Telecommunications | V2X becomes a new revenue vertical (5G, C-V2X) | | Logistics & Supply Chain | Truck platooning reduces fuel use by 10–15% via air-drag reduction | | Road Safety Engineering | Targets elimination of >90% of crash fatalities | | Environmental Engineering | Eco-platooning reduces CO₂ emissions per ton-km | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Derivation: Why Platooning Multiplies Highway Capacity This is a **classic 7-mark KTU derivation** that combines kinematics with traffic-flow theory. ### Step 1 — Define fundamental capacity The maximum flow of a single highway lane is given by the **fluid analogy of traffic**: $$q = \frac{v}{s}$$ Where: - $q$ = flow (vehicles per hour per lane, veh/hr/lane) - $v$ = mean operating speed (km/h) - $s$ = mean spacing between successive vehicles (m) ### Step 2 — Express spacing in terms of headway For a vehicle traveling at speed $v$ (in m/s), the spacing is: $$s = v \cdot t_h + L_{veh}$$ Where $t_h$ is the time headway (s) and $L_{veh}$ is the vehicle length (≈ 5 m). ### Step 3 — Convert speed to km/h Let $V$ = speed in km/h. Then $v = V/3.6$ m/s. Substituting: $$s = \frac{V \cdot t_h}{3.6} + L_{veh}$$ ### Step 4 — Plug into capacity equation $$q = \frac{V}{s} = \frac{V}{\dfrac{V \cdot t_h}{3.6} + L_{veh}}$$ Multiply numerator and denominator by 3.6: $$q = \frac{3.6 \cdot V}{V \cdot t_h + 3.6 \cdot L_{veh}}$$ ### Step 5 — Evaluate for conventional driving Assume $V = 100$ km/h, $t_h = 1.8$ s (human), $L_{veh} = 5$ m: $$q_{conv} = \frac{3.6 \times 100}{100 \times 1.8 + 3.6 \times 5} = \frac{360}{180 + 18} = \frac{360}{198} \approx 1818 \text{ veh/hr/lane}$$ ### Step 6 — Evaluate for AHS / CACC platooning With CACC, $t_h \approx 0.6$ s: $$q_{CACC} = \frac{3.6 \times 100}{100 \times 0.6 + 3.6 \times 5} = \frac{360}{60 + 18} = \frac{360}{78} \approx 4615 \text{ veh/hr/lane}$$ ### Step 7 — Evaluate for tight platooning With $t_h = 0.3$ s: $$q_{tight} = \frac{360}{30 + 18} = \frac{360}{48} = 7500 \text{ veh/hr/lane}$$ ### Step 8 — Capacity Multiplication Factor $$\text{CMF} = \frac{q_{AHS}}{q_{conv}} = \frac{4615}{1818} \approx 2.54\times$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result to remember:** AHS through CACC can increase lane capacity by a factor of **2.5 to 4×** compared to a human-driven lane, *without adding a single new lane of road*. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: Safe Following Distance Computation A CACC-equipped truck travels at $v = 25$ m/s (90 km/h). If the time headway is $t_h = 0.6$ s and the standstill gap is $d_0 = 2$ m, compute the safe following distance. ### Step 1 — Identify given values - $v = 25$ m/s - $t_h = 0.6$ s - $d_0 = 2$ m ### Step 2 — Apply safe-distance formula $$d_{safe} = v \cdot t_h + d_0$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute $$d_{safe} = 25 \times 0.6 + 2 = 15 + 2 = 17 \text{ m}$$ ### Step 4 — Compare with human driver For a human at $t_h = 1.8$ s, $d_0 = 2$ m: $$d_{human} = 25 \times 1.8 + 2 = 45 + 2 = 47 \text{ m}$$ ### Step 5 — Gap reduction $$\Delta d = 47 - 17 = 30 \text{ m saved per vehicle pair}$$ > [!NOTE] > This saved 30 m per pair, multiplied across a platoon of 20 trucks, frees up ~600 m of highway per convoy — directly translating to the **capacity increase** derived in Section 3.1. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: String-Stability of a Platoon A platoon of 4 vehicles uses a constant-spacing policy with $d_{gap} = 8$ m. The lead vehicle brakes with deceleration $a_0 = 3$ m/s². The controller gain is $K = 0.7$ s⁻¹. Determine the deceleration of the 4th (last) vehicle. ### Step 1 — String-stability condition For a constant-spacing platoon to remain string-stable, the spacing-error feedback must satisfy: $$\vert G(j\omega) \vert < 1 \quad \forall \omega$$ A simplified first-order model gives the *nth* vehicle's deceleration as: $$a_n = a_0 \cdot K^{n-1}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute values $$a_4 = 3 \cdot (0.7)^{4-1} = 3 \cdot (0.7)^3$$ ### Step 3 — Calculate the cube of 0.7 $$(0.7)^3 = 0.7 \times 0.7 \times 0.7 = 0.49 \times 0.7 = 0.343$$ ### Step 4 — Final deceleration $$a_4 = 3 \times 0.343 = 1.029 \text{ m/s}^2$$ ### Step 5 — Interpretation The last vehicle brakes at ~1.03 m/s² — well within the comfortable braking envelope of <2.5 m/s². **The platoon is string-stable.** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's trap:** If students blindly use $a_n = a_0 \cdot K$ for every vehicle, they will incorrectly get $a_4 = 3 \times 0.7 = 2.1$ m/s². The correct cascaded model is $K^{n-1}$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Technology Stack of an AHS | Layer | Hardware / Software | Function | Critical Performance Metric | |---|---|---|---| | Sensing | 77 GHz Radar | Forward obstacle ranging | Range ≤ 200 m, refresh 20 Hz | | Sensing | 360° LiDAR | 3D environment mapping | Angular resolution 0.1° | | Sensing | GNSS + IMU | Localization | Position accuracy ≤ 0.1 m | | Comms | DSRC / C-V2X | V2V / V2I messaging | Latency ≤ 100 ms | | Comms | 5G URLLC | Backbone for cloud AHS | Latency ≤ 1 ms | | Control | Electronic Throttle Controller | Longitudinal actuation | Response ≤ 50 ms | | Control | EPS (Electric Power Steering) | Lateral actuation | Response ≤ 30 ms | | Software | HD Maps | Lane-level path planning | Accuracy ≤ 10 cm | | Software | Sensor Fusion (Kalman Filter) | Unified world model | Update 100 Hz | | Software | Path Planner (RRT*, MPC) | Trajectory generation | Horizon ≥ 5 s | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 AHS Functional Architecture — Block Diagram The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **end-to-end functional flow** of an Automated Highway System, mapping how sensor inputs, communication, decision algorithms, and actuators interlock. ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Roadway Infrastructure
Magnetic Markers, Beacons, RSUs] A2[Onboard Sensors
Radar, LiDAR, Camera, GNSS IMU] A3[Neighbouring Vehicles
V2V Data, Platoon State] B1[V2I Communication
DSRC, C-V2X, 5G] B2[Sensor Fusion Engine
Kalman Filter, Object Detection] C1[Perception Module
Object List, HD Map Localisation] C2[Behaviour Layer
Lane Change, Merge Decision] D1[Motion Planner
Trajectory MPC Optimisation] D2[Longitudinal Controller
Throttle Brake Actuator] D3[Lateral Controller
Electric Power Steering] E1[Vehicle Actuators
Throttle, Brake, Steering] E2[HMI Display
Driver Status, Alerts] F1[Supervisory Coordinator
Platoon Manager, ODD Watchdog] A1 --> B1 A2 --> B2 A3 --> B1 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D1 --> D3 D2 --> E1 D3 --> E1 E1 --> A3 C2 --> F1 F1 --> D1 F1 --> E2 ``` --- ## 4.2 AHS Communication Topology — V2X Ecosystem This diagram shows how V2V, V2I, and V2P (Pedestrian) interact in an AHS environment. ```mermaid flowchart LR HV[Host Vehicle
AHS Equipped] -->|V2V| V1[Vehicle Ahead] HV -->|V2V| V2[Vehicle Behind] HV -->|V2I| RSU[Roadside Unit
Traffic Signal Beacon] HV -->|V2P| PD[Pedestrian Smartphone] RSU -->|Fiber Backhaul| CC[Traffic Management Centre] CC -->|Cloud Analytics| HV HV -->|V2N 5G| MEC[Multi access Edge Compute] MEC -->|HD Map Updates| HV ``` --- ## 4.3 Vehicle Automation Level Decision Tree (SAE J3016) ```mermaid flowchart TB S0[Start: Is driving task automated?] S0 -->|No| L0[Level 0 - No Automation] S0 -->|Yes| Q1{Is lateral or longitudinal
control automated?} Q1 -->|Yes| L1[Level 1 - Driver Assistance] Q1 -->|Both| Q2{Does system monitor
driving environment?} Q2 -->|No| L2[Level 2 - Partial Automation] Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Does system handle
fallback performance?} Q3 -->|No| L3[Level 3 - Conditional Automation] Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Is system restricted
to a geofenced ODD?} Q4 -->|Yes| L4[Level 4 - High Automation
AHS Lane] Q4 -->|No| L5[Level 5 - Full Automation
Theoretical Full AHS] ``` --- ## 4.4 AHS Operational Sequence — Lane Entry to Exit ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Driver participant HMI participant AHS_Controller as AHS Controller participant RSU as Roadside Unit participant Platoon as Platoon Leader Driver->>HMI: Request AHS activation HMI->>AHS_Controller: Check ODD validity AHS_Controller->>RSU: Request lane entry clearance RSU-->>AHS_Controller: Lane open, join slot 3 AHS_Controller->>Platoon: V2V join request Platoon-->>AHS_Controller: Accept, gap forming AHS_Controller->>Driver: Take hands off wheel Note over AHS_Controller,Platoon: Longitudinal + Lateral control engaged AHS_Controller->>RSU: Approaching exit RSU-->>AHS_Controller: Hand off to driver in 800 m AHS_Controller->>Driver: Warning - resume manual Driver->>AHS_Controller: Manual control acknowledged AHS_Controller->>Platoon: Leave platoon ``` --- ## 4.5 Sensor Fusion Architecture (Perception Layer) ```mermaid flowchart TB R[77 GHz Radar
Range, Velocity] --> F1[Kalman Filter Fusion] L[LiDAR
3D Point Cloud] --> F1 C[Cameras
Image, Lanes, Signs] --> F1 G[GNSS + IMU
Position, Heading] --> F1 F1 --> O[Object List
Cars, Trucks, Pedestrians] F1 --> LM[Local Map
Lanes, Curb, Signals] O --> P[Path Planner] LM --> P P --> CTRL[Vehicle Controller] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define Automated Highway System (AHS). Mention any two of its benefits. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 model]** **Model Answer:** An Automated Highway System (AHS) is a system in which intelligent, instrumented vehicles operate on dedicated or specially equipped highway lanes, performing longitudinal (throttle, brake) and lateral (steering) control automatically using sensors, actuators, and V2V/V2I communication, with the aim of improving safety, increasing capacity, and reducing driver workload. **Two benefits:** 1. **Increased lane capacity** — Through tight vehicle platooning with reduced headways (0.3–0.6 s), a single lane can carry 2.5–4× more vehicles per hour than a conventional lane. 2. **Enhanced safety** — Removing the human driver eliminates errors from fatigue, distraction, and slow reaction times, potentially preventing 90%+ of crash fatalities caused by human factors. --- ### Q2. List the four functional layers of an AHS architecture and briefly explain the role of the Control Layer. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 model]** **Model Answer:** The four functional layers of an AHS are: 1. **Perception Layer** — Acquires data on environment (vehicles, lanes, obstacles) using radar, LiDAR, cameras, and GNSS. 2. **Communication Layer** — Exchanges data between vehicles (V2V) and infrastructure (V2I) using DSRC, C-V2X, or 5G. 3. **Control Layer** — Processes the perceived information and commands the vehicle's actuators. 4. **Coordination Layer** — Manages higher-level decisions such as lane changes, merging, and platoon formation. **Role of the Control Layer:** It is the "brain" of the AHS, split into **Longitudinal Control** (throttle and brake actuation to maintain headway and platoon spacing) and **Lateral Control** (steering actuation to keep the vehicle within lane boundaries). It uses feedback control algorithms (PID, Model Predictive Control) operating on sensor inputs to maintain sub-centimetre precision in vehicle motion. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B Module questions carry **internal choice**. Both alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) are given below. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Platooning, Capacity & Communication **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 model]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of an Automated Highway System (AHS)**. List the four functional layers and the role of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **capacity multiplication factor** of an AHS lane over a conventional lane for a constant speed of 100 km/h, given that human-driven headway $t_h = 1.8$ s, CACC headway $t_h = 0.6$ s, and vehicle length $L = 5$ m. Discuss the role of **DSRC** in supporting platoon coordination. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram of AHS Functional Architecture** The four layers are: - **Perception Layer:** Radar, LiDAR, camera, GNSS/IMU acquire raw data on surroundings. - **Communication Layer:** V2V (DSRC, IEEE 802.11p) and V2I (RSUs, traffic management centre) exchange data. - **Control Layer:** Longitudinal (throttle/brake) and lateral (steering) controllers maintain vehicle motion. - **Coordination Layer:** High-level logic for lane-changing, merging, and platoon formation. *(Block diagram: see Section 4.1 of these notes — show the layered flow from sensors → fusion → perception → planning → control → actuators → vehicle.)* **(b) Capacity Derivation** As derived in **Section 3.1**, the per-lane capacity is: $$q = \frac{3.6 \cdot V}{V \cdot t_h + 3.6 \cdot L_{veh}}$$ **Conventional (human) lane:** $V = 100$ km/h, $t_h = 1.8$ s, $L = 5$ m $$q_{conv} = \frac{360}{100 \cdot 1.8 + 3.6 \cdot 5} = \frac{360}{180 + 18} = \frac{360}{198} = 1818 \text{ veh/hr/lane}$$ **AHS lane (CACC):** $t_h = 0.6$ s $$q_{CACC} = \frac{360}{100 \cdot 0.6 + 3.6 \cdot 5} = \frac{360}{60 + 18} = \frac{360}{78} = 4615 \text{ veh/hr/lane}$$ **Capacity Multiplication Factor:** $$\text{CMF} = \frac{4615}{1818} = 2.54$$ So an AHS lane carries **~2.54× more vehicles per hour** than a conventional lane at the same speed. **Role of DSRC:** Dedicated Short-Range Communications (DSRC, IEEE 802.11p) operates in the 5.9 GHz band and provides: - **Low latency (≤ 100 ms)** for safety-critical messages (emergency brake, collision warning). - **V2V range of ~300 m**, sufficient to coordinate a 10–15 vehicle platoon. - **Bilateral data sharing** of speed, acceleration, and position, enabling the CACC controller to reduce time-headway safely to 0.6 s. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Sensors, Levels of Automation & AHS **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 model]** **(a)** Describe the **NHTSA/SAE J3016 levels of vehicle automation**. Identify the level that corresponds to an AHS-equipped vehicle and justify your answer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain with a diagram the **sensor fusion architecture** used in an AHS perception layer. Discuss why **77 GHz mmWave radar** is preferred over 24 GHz radar for AHS applications. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) SAE J3016 Automation Levels** *(Table content from Section 2.1 of these notes — Levels 0 through 5.)* **AHS corresponds to Level 4 (High Automation):** - **Justification:** In AHS, the system performs *all* longitudinal and lateral control *and* monitors the driving environment *and* handles fallback performance (e.g., safe stop) without human intervention. However, this is **restricted to a defined Operational Design Domain (ODD)** — namely, the instrumented highway lane. Outside this ODD, the system cannot operate, which exactly matches the Level 4 definition. - Level 5 (Full Automation, any road, any weather) is the *theoretical* extension of AHS but is not yet commercially realised. **(b) Sensor Fusion Architecture** *(Diagram: see Section 4.5 of these notes — Radar, LiDAR, Camera, GNSS/IMU all feeding into a Kalman Filter fusion engine that produces an Object List and Local Map for the Path Planner.)* **Why 77 GHz mmWave radar is preferred:** Using the radar range equation $P_r \propto \lambda^2 / R^4$, the ratio of received power at 77 GHz vs 24 GHz for the same target range is: $$\frac{P_{r,77}}{P_{r,24}} = \left(\frac{\lambda_{77}}{\lambda_{24}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{c/f_{77}}{c/f_{24}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{24}{77}\right)^2 \approx 0.097$$ This is a *power-loss* factor. However, the **antenna gain** at 77 GHz is also higher (proportional to $1/\lambda^2$), which compensates. The net benefits of 77 GHz for AHS are: - **Wider bandwidth (~4 GHz vs ~250 MHz)** → finer range resolution (~4 cm vs ~60 cm), critical for distinguishing two closely-spaced vehicles in a platoon. - **Smaller antenna size** for the same beamwidth → compact form factor suitable for vehicle integration. - **Better Doppler resolution** → more accurate relative-velocity measurement for car-following control. - **Higher immunity to interference** from other 24 GHz consumer devices. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make in AHS questions — read carefully:** > 1. **Confusing ACC with CACC** — ACC uses *only* onboard radar (headway ~1.4 s). CACC uses *both* radar and V2V communication (headway ~0.6 s). Many students mark them as the same. > 2. **Quoting wrong capacity numbers** — Some textbooks cite 6000 veh/hr/lane for AHS; this is for *tight* platooning ($t_h \approx 0.3$ s). At realistic CACC headway, the figure is ~4500–5000 veh/hr/lane. > 3. **Mixing up DSRC and C-V2X** — DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) is Wi-Fi-derived; C-V2X (3GPP) is cellular-derived. Both use the 5.9 GHz band but have different physical layers. > 4. **Forgetting the ODD constraint** — A Level 4 system is restricted to a defined ODD (highway, geofence). Students who claim "Level 4 = any road" lose 2 marks. > 5. **Skipping units in derivations** — Always carry units through your capacity derivation (m/s ↔ km/h conversion is a common source of arithmetic error). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for AHS:** - **Definition:** AHS = automated vehicles + instrumented highway + V2V/V2I communication → fully self-driving on a defined lane. - **SAE Levels:** Memorize 0–5. AHS = **Level 4 (high automation, ODD-bounded)**. - **Four Layers:** Perception → Communication → Control → Coordination. - **Two Control Loops:** Longitudinal (throttle, brake) and Lateral (steering). - **ACC vs CACC:** ACC = radar only, 1.4 s headway. CACC = radar + V2V, 0.6 s headway. - **Platooning:** Strings of vehicles at 0.3–0.6 s headway and 4–10 m gaps. **String stability** is the key safety property. - **Capacity Multiplication:** AHS lane = **2.5–4×** conventional lane capacity. Derive using $q = 360V / (V \cdot t_h + 3.6 \cdot L)$. - **Safe Distance Formula:** $d_{safe} = v \cdot t_h + d_0$. - **Sensors:** Camera + Radar + LiDAR + GNSS/IMU. **77 GHz radar** preferred for fine range resolution. - **Communication:** DSRC (IEEE 802.11p, ~100 ms latency) and C-V2X (3GPP, 5G-evolution). Latency target for safety messages: **≤ 100 ms**. - **Radar Equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma / [(4\pi)^3 R^4 L]$ — the $R^4$ term dictates maximum detection range. - **Demonstration:** USDOT NAHSC Demo '97 in San Diego was the landmark public AHS demonstration (8 automated vehicles in a platoon). - **Benefits:** Safety (eliminates human error), Capacity (2.5–4×), Comfort (no driving stress), Environment (eco-platooning saves 10–15% fuel in trucks). - **Challenges:** Cybersecurity of V2X, mixed-traffic transition, legal liability, weather edge cases, HD map freshness. - **Standards to quote in exams:** SAE J3016, IEEE 802.11p, ISO 26262 (functional safety), 3GPP C-V2X Rel. 14.

topics.title: Connected Vehicle System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET721 - INTELLIGENT TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Connected Vehicle System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Connected Vehicle System — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connected Vehicle System (CVS)** is an advanced Intelligent Transportation System (ITS) framework that leverages **wireless communication technologies**, **on-board sensors**, and **edge/cloud computing** to enable real-time data exchange between vehicles, infrastructure, pedestrians, and networks. The overarching goal is to dramatically improve **road safety**, **transportation mobility**, and **environmental sustainability** by transforming isolated vehicles into cooperative, information-rich agents within a cyber-physical transportation ecosystem.

According to the **U.S. Department of Transportation (USDOT)** and the **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE)**, a connected vehicle utilizes **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X)** communication — an umbrella term encompassing **V2V** (Vehicle-to-Vehicle), **V2I** (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure), **V2P** (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian), **V2N** (Vehicle-to-Network), and **V2G** (Vehicle-to-Grid) — operating primarily in the **5.85–5.925 GHz** spectrum band.

### Key Conceptual Hierarchy

| Term | Scope | Typical Range | Latency |
|------|-------|---------------|---------|
| **V2V** | Vehicle ↔ Vehicle | ~300 m | <20 ms |
| **V2I** | Vehicle ↔ Roadside Unit | ~1 km | <100 ms |
| **V2P** | Vehicle ↔ Pedestrian (smartphone) | ~50–100 m | <100 ms |
| **V2N** | Vehicle ↔ Cellular Cloud | Cellular coverage | 50–200 ms |
| **V2G** | Vehicle ↔ Power Grid | Bidirectional charging | Variable |
| **V2X** | All of the above | Technology-agnostic | <100 ms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** Connected Vehicle Systems form the *communication backbone* of modern ITS. Mastery of **DSRC vs. C-V2X**, **OBU/RSU architecture**, and **V2X message standards (SAE J2735, J2945, ETSI EN 302 637)** is mandatory for the ESE (End Semester Examination).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Talking Traffic"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a city where every car, traffic signal, and pedestrian's smartphone can "talk" to each other in real time.** A Connected Vehicle System is like giving every road user a *group chat*. Before a collision happens, two cars exchange their speed and position; before a red light, the signal warns an approaching car to slow down smoothly. The vehicle becomes *contextually aware* beyond line-of-sight — it can "see" a braking car hidden behind a truck, anticipate a jaywalking pedestrian, and reroute around a congestion hotspot, *all through radio whispers*.

Just as **WhatsApp transformed human communication** by making every phone a node in a global mesh, **V2X transforms every vehicle into a node in a transportation mesh** — eliminating the *information asymmetry* that causes most road accidents.

---

## 1.3 Why Connected Vehicle Systems Matter — The Three Pillars

1. **Safety Pillar** — Mitigates the **>90% of accidents caused by human error** (NHTSA data) by extending perception beyond line-of-sight and reaction-time limitations.
2. **Mobility Pillar** — Reduces congestion, enables **eco-platooning**, and supports **Green Light Optimal Speed Advisory (GLOSA)**, cutting commute times by **15–30%**.
3. **Environmental Pillar** — Optimizes driving patterns to reduce **CO₂ emissions by up to 15%** and fuel consumption through cooperative adaptive cruise control (C-ACC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants Used in CVS:**
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
> - Boltzmann's constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
> - DSRC center frequency: $f_c = 5.9 \text{ GHz}$
> - Standard vehicle speed reference: $v = 60 \text{ km/h} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}$
> - Earth surface temperature (for noise calculations): $T_0 = 290 \text{ K}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of a Connected Vehicle System

A Connected Vehicle System is structured into **four interlocking layers**, each fulfilling a distinct functional role:

### Layer 1 — Perception Layer (Sensor Fusion)
- **Sensors:** Radar, LiDAR, cameras, ultrasonic, IMU.
- **Localization:** GPS (typical accuracy ±2–5 m), augmented with **RTK-GNSS** for centimeter-level precision.
- **Function:** Collects raw kinematic state (position, velocity, heading, acceleration) and environmental context.

### Layer 2 — Communication Layer (V2X Radio)
- **DSRC** (IEEE 802.11p / WAVE) — short-range, low-latency, ad-hoc.
- **C-V2X** (3GPP Release 14/15/16, 5G NR-V2X) — cellular-based with sidelink (PC5).
- **Function:** Transmits **Basic Safety Messages (BSM)** / **Cooperative Awareness Messages (CAM)** at **10 Hz**.

### Layer 3 — Data Processing & Edge Layer
- **On-Board Unit (OBU)** executes local V2X applications.
- **Roadside Unit (RSU)** performs edge analytics and forwards data to Traffic Management Centers (TMC).
- **Function:** Local decision-making with sub-100 ms response.

### Layer 4 — Application & Cloud Layer
- Back-office servers, **MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing)**, and cloud platforms host non-safety applications.
- **Function:** Aggregated analytics, HD map updates, fleet management, software OTA (Over-The-Air).

---

## 2.2 Communication Technologies — DSRC vs. C-V2X

| Parameter | **DSRC (IEEE 802.11p)** | **C-V2X (3GPP Rel. 14+)** |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| Spectrum | **5.85–5.925 GHz** (75 MHz, US) | 5.9 GHz (PC5 sidelink) + licensed cellular |
| Standard Family | IEEE 802.11p + IEEE 1609.x | 3GPP Release 14, 15, 16 (5G NR) |
| Modulation | OFDM (52 subcarriers) | OFDM / SC-FDM |
| Bandwidth | **10 MHz / 20 MHz** channels | 10/20 MHz (sidelink); wider for 5G |
| Data Rate | 6 – 27 Mbps | Up to **1 Gbps (5G NR-V2X)** |
| Latency | < 10 ms | ~5 ms (5G) |
| Range | ~300 m (line-of-sight) | ~400+ m (with cellular coverage extension) |
| Synchronization | GPS required | GNSS + cellular sync |
| Network Mode | Ad-hoc (no infrastructure) | Both PC5 (sidelink) and Uu (network) |
| Maturity | Mature (deployed in US/EU pilots) | Rapidly evolving (5G-AA, China) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** In Module 4 viva questions, examiners frequently ask *“Compare DSRC and C-V2X with at least four distinguishing parameters.”* Memorize the **spectrum, latency, data rate, and range** as the four non-negotiable columns.

---

## 2.3 On-Board Unit (OBU) and Roadside Unit (RSU) — Hardware Anatomy

### OBU Functional Stack
1. **Application Layer** — Safety/Mobility/Infotainment apps.
2. **Message Sublayer** — SAE J2735 message encoding (BSM, MAP, SPaT).
3. **Networking Layer** — IEEE 1609.3 (WAVE Short Message Protocol – WSMP).
4. **MAC/PHY Layer** — IEEE 802.11p radio.
5. **Security Layer** — IEEE 1609.2 (signed message authentication using **ECDSA – Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm**).
6. **Hardware** — ARM Cortex-A series processor, GNSS receiver, dedicated crypto chip.

### RSU Functional Stack
- Same layers as OBU **plus**:
  - **Backhaul Interface** — Ethernet / Fiber / 4G/5G.
  - **Power Supply** — Mains or solar with battery backup.
  - **Mounting** — Gantries, signal poles, or standalone poles.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE Module 4 problems. Memorize the *form*, *units*, and *physical meaning* of each.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Units |
|---|-----------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{FSPL}(dB) = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$ | Free-Space Path Loss (Friis) | dB |
| 2 | $\text{FSPL}(dB) = 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$ | Practical FSPL (d in km, f in MHz) | dB |
| 3 | $P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Friis Transmission Equation | W |
| 4 | $P_r(dBm) = P_t(dBm) + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} - L_{misc}$ | Received Power (link budget) | dBm |
| 5 | $N = k \, T_0 \, B$ | Thermal Noise Power | W |
| 6 | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_r}{N} = \dfrac{P_r}{k T_0 B}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon–Hartley Channel Capacity | bps |
| 8 | $C_{\text{throughput}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{packet}} \times 8}{T_{\text{transmit}}}$ | Effective Throughput | bps |
| 9 | $d_{\max} = \dfrac{c \cdot T_p}{2}$ | Maximum Detectable Range (radar) | m |
| 10 | $\tau_{\text{total}} = \tau_{proc} + \tau_{queue} + \tau_{tx} + \tau_{prop}$ | End-to-End Latency | s |
| 11 | $L_{prop} = \tau_{prop} \cdot v$ | Longitudinal Position Error (moved vehicle during propagation) | m |
| 12 | $f_{BSM} = 10 \text{ Hz}$ (i.e., $T_{BSM} = 100 \text{ ms}$) | BSM transmission rate | Hz |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations underpin **link budget design** (covering range estimation), **latency budget design** (covering safety-critical timing), and **capacity planning** for V2X deployments. They appear in **Part B 14-mark numericals** with high probability.

---

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Layer

- **Why 5.9 GHz?** — The **5.85–5.925 GHz band** was allocated globally by the ITU because it balances *propagation characteristics* (good penetration through rain/foliage) with *available bandwidth* (~75 MHz in US) and *short-range suitability* for safety messages.
- **Why 10 Hz BSM?** — At **100 km/h**, a vehicle travels **2.78 m per 100 ms**. The 10 Hz frequency ensures the message is fresh enough to avoid *stale-data collisions* in cooperative awareness algorithms.
- **Why 802.11p and not 5G Wi-Fi?** — IEEE 802.11p uses **Outside the Context of a BSS (OCB)** mode, eliminating the need for association/authentication handshake — critical for *ad-hoc vehicular networks* where two cars have only ~5 seconds of contact at highway speeds.
- **Why ECDSA security?** — A forged BSM could trigger phantom braking. **ECDSA with 256-bit keys** provides cryptographic authenticity in < 5 ms — vital for V2X trust.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control (C-ACC)** — Trucks platooning at **0.3 s gap**, reducing fuel consumption by **~10%**.
2. **Intersection Movement Assist (IMA)** — Prevents **~40% of intersection crashes** (USDOT pilot data).
3. **Emergency Electronic Brake Light (EEBL)** — Warns trailing vehicles of hard-braking events hidden by obstructions.
4. **Vulnerable Road User (VRU) Protection** — Detects cyclists/pedestrians via smartphone V2P.
5. **Smart Parking** — RSU aggregates parking availability, directs drivers to nearest vacant spot.
6. **Road Weather Alerts** — RSU broadcasts localized black-ice/fog warnings.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section presents the **exhaustive derivations** of the core equations in the KTU Formula Sheet. Each algebraic step is documented with a textual explanation row for maximum clarity. Use this section as your **problem-solving template** for ESE numericals.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) from First Principles

### Goal
Derive the expression for **path loss in free space** for a V2X link between a transmitting OBU and a receiving RSU, given transmitted power, antenna gains, frequency, and distance.

### Given
- Transmitted power: $P_t$ (W)
- Transmit antenna gain: $G_t$ (linear)
- Receive antenna gain: $G_r$ (linear)
- Frequency: $f$ (Hz)
- Distance: $d$ (m)
- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define Power Flux Density at Distance d**

The transmit antenna concentrates radiated power $P_t \, G_t$ over a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$.

$$S = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad \left[\frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^2}\right]$$

*Explanation:* This is the **Poynting vector magnitude** — the power crossing unit area perpendicular to propagation at distance $d$.

**Step 2 — Effective Aperture of Receive Antenna**

A receive antenna of gain $G_r$ has an effective aperture:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{4\pi} \quad [\text{m}^2]$$

*Explanation:* Larger aperture $\Rightarrow$ more energy captured. Aperture scales with the **square of the wavelength** $\lambda$.

**Step 3 — Combine to Get Received Power**

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

Simplify:

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{\!2} \quad [\text{W}]$$

This is the **Friis Transmission Equation**.

**Step 4 — Express Path Loss as the Ratio $P_t / P_r$**

$$\text{PL} = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \frac{1}{G_t G_r} \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{\!2}$$

For **isotropic antennas** ($G_t = G_r = 1$):

$$\text{FSPL} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{\!2}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Decibels**

$$\text{FSPL}(dB) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{\!2} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\lambda = c / f$**

$$\text{FSPL}(dB) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Practical Form (d in km, f in MHz)**

Using $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} = 3 \times 10^5 \text{ km/s}$ and unit conversions:

$$\boxed{\;\text{FSPL}(dB) = 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44\;}$$

**Step 8 — Verification with Numerical Example**

For a DSRC link at $f = 5900 \text{ MHz}$ and $d = 0.3 \text{ km}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL} &= 20 \log_{10}(0.3) + 20 \log_{10}(5900) + 32.44 \\
&= 20 \times (-0.5229) + 20 \times 3.771 + 32.44 \\
&= -10.46 + 75.42 + 32.44 \\
&= 97.40 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* A signal transmitted at **+20 dBm (100 mW)** suffers ~97.4 dB loss over 300 m, leaving a received power of:

$$P_r = 20 - 97.4 = -77.4 \text{ dBm} \;(\approx 1.8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mW})$$

This is **above the DSRC sensitivity of –92 dBm**, leaving a margin of ~14.6 dB for fading and interference.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — End-to-End Latency Budget for a BSM

### Goal
Compute the **maximum allowable end-to-end latency** for a safety-critical BSM to remain valid for cooperative collision avoidance.

### Given
- Vehicle speed: $v$ (m/s)
- BSM message size: $L_p$ (bits)
- Channel data rate: $R$ (bps)
- Processing time at OBU: $\tau_{proc}$ (s)
- Propagation time over distance $d$: $\tau_{prop}$ (s)
- Queueing delay: $\tau_{queue}$ (s)

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Transmission Time**

The transmitter needs:

$$\tau_{tx} = \frac{L_p}{R} \quad [\text{s}]$$

For a typical BSM of **300 bytes = 2400 bits** at **6 Mbps**:

$$\tau_{tx} = \frac{2400}{6 \times 10^6} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.4 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation Time**

$$\tau_{prop} = \frac{d}{c}$$

For $d = 300 \text{ m}$:

$$\tau_{prop} = \frac{300}{3 \times 10^8} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 3 — Total Latency (excluding queueing)**

$$\tau_{total} = \tau_{proc} + \tau_{tx} + \tau_{prop}$$

Assuming $\tau_{proc} = 5 \text{ ms}$:

$$\tau_{total} = 5 + 0.4 + 0.001 \approx 5.401 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Position Error During Latency**

At $v = 27.78 \text{ m/s}$ (100 km/h), the vehicle moves:

$$L_{prop} = \tau_{total} \times v = 5.401 \times 10^{-3} \times 27.78 = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Acceptable Latency (Safety Constraint)**

For cooperative collision avoidance, the position error should not exceed **0.5 m**:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{L_{max}}{v} = \frac{0.5}{27.78} \approx 18 \text{ ms}$$

This is the **upper bound** the entire CVS communication stack must respect.

**Step 6 — Final Latency Budget Equation**

$$\boxed{\;\tau_{total} = \tau_{proc} + \tau_{queue} + \frac{L_p}{R} + \frac{d}{c} \leq 18 \text{ ms}\;}$$

*Interpretation:* This 18 ms ceiling drives the design of **5G NR-V2X** (5 ms one-way air interface latency) and aggressive edge computing in the OBU.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Channel Capacity & Throughput Trade-Off

### Goal
Determine the maximum achievable V2X throughput and compare it to the demand of an autonomous vehicle's sensor stream.

### Given
- Bandwidth: $B = 10 \text{ MHz}$ (DSRC channel)
- Received SNR: 20 dB (linear = 100)

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Shannon–Hartley Theorem**

$$C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 10^7 \times \log_2(1 + 100)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 10^7 \times \log_2(101) \\
&= 10^7 \times 6.658 \\
&= 6.658 \times 10^7 \text{ bps} \approx 66.58 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare to DSRC Realistic Data Rate**

DSRC with 64-QAM 1/2 in 10 MHz channel achieves **27 Mbps** — only **~40% of Shannon capacity**, which is typical for practical OFDM systems.

**Step 3 — Autonomous Vehicle Sensor Demand**

| Sensor | Raw Data Rate |
|--------|---------------|
| 4 × Cameras (4K) | 4,000 Mbps |
| 1 × LiDAR (128-channel) | 1,000 Mbps |
| Radar (8 units) | 200 Mbps |
| **Total** | **~5,200 Mbps** |

**Step 4 — Conclusion**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{V2X uplink (BSM)} &= 0.024 \text{ Mbps (300 bytes @ 10 Hz)} \\
\text{V2X downlink (MAP/SPaT)} &= 0.5 \text{ Mbps} \\
\text{Sensor total} &= 5,200 \text{ Mbps (off-board)} \\
\text{V2X ratio} &= \dfrac{0.524}{5200} \approx 0.01\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Engineering Implication:* **V2X carries only cooperative awareness metadata, NOT raw sensor data.** This is why DSRC/C-V2X bandwidth is sufficient for safety — but the autonomous vehicle must process its own sensor stack locally.
